raw_recent_text: "Ugh, not again," you spout as the old wagon shutters, then dies.
Stranded on the road again, but this time, your money has gone dry.
All but your 50 dollar bill that Grandma gave you on her last Christmas.
You've been saving it for when you needed it most, but surely, it won't be enough.
The door creaks open, and you step out into the night sky, coughing up the smoke from your fried vehicle.
After pushing your car off the road and between the trees, there isn't much else left for you to do,
so you begin to wander down the dark, lonely street.
But at the end of the road, where concrete turned to stone turned to dirt, you notice a light up ahead, on the top of a hill.
As you waltz into the old, wooden shack, your eyes begin to light up with the fire of a thousand suns.
Roulette wheels! Poker tables! And in a dark corner of the abandoned casino, sits a dealer, shuffling cards for a new round of Blackjack.
That 50 dollars might just come in handy after all. Thanks, Grandma!
As you go to sit down at the table, you hear the Dealer cough, then watch as he sits up.
In a deep, and yet strained voice, the Dealer, cloaked in darkness, poses a question to you.
Would you like to play a game of Blackjack?
You have $50

"Stars aren't far away. They're just really small. They're so small that all 17 of them could fit into the earth. That's why we can't get to them. They move away so the planet doesn't eat them and only show up at night when the earth is sleeping."

and an old man with tattoo sleeves and long black hair steps out.
He kicks his car, and the engine starts blaring once more.
"Hello, the name's Frank. Now I've got a baseball game to catch, but it looks like you could use some help."
Frank pulls a shiny silver hammer out of his pocket, and walks to the hood of your beaten down wagon.
"My god. This is just awful. Tell you what, I can fix this up for like 100 bucks,
and your engine will be runnin' just as good as mine. You game?"
"What?! How could you not accept my service? I'm the cheapest damn autoshop worker on this here planet!
But NOOOO, NOT FRANK! Never Frank. He Voted For Trump!
Let's all ridicule frank for his political party. You god damn liberals."
Frank spits in your face, and get back in his truck.
You watch as he revs his engine, gets out of his truck, kicks his beater, gets back in, revs his engine, and speeds off into the horizon.
After wandering from your vehicle, you find yourself deep in the woods.
Squirrels run by and up into the trees. The sun hits every branch and casts a shadow below.
And you wander on a natural path, journeying into the unknown.
You walk, and walk, and walk further down the path, before the forest opens up to the main road.
You follow the road back to your wagon, wondering if there was anything you missed.
At least you made it back safe and sound.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
The table is set. The cards are ready. Are you?

You're browsing the chips when the door slams open.
"EVERYBODY ON THE GROUND! NOW!"
A man with a shotgun. Ski mask. Shaking hands. This is really happening.
The cashier freezes. A mother clutches her child. An old man drops his coffee.
The robber swings the gun around, wild-eyed.
"EVERYBODY ON THE GROUND! NOW!"
A man with a shotgun. Ski mask. Shaking hands. This is really happening.
The cashier freezes. A mother clutches her child. An old man drops his coffee.
The robber swings the gun around, wild-eyed.
You duck behind a shelf. Slowly, carefully, you crawl toward the back.
The robber is focused on the register. You're almost to the storage room...
A chip bag crinkles under your knee. The robber spins.
"I SEE YOU! GET OUT HERE!"
The shotgun is pointed right at you. You raise your hands and step out.
"Think you're smart? Think you can hide from me?"
He hits you with the stock. Your vision explodes into stars.
Your mind feels... foggy. Something isn't right.
The cards in your dreams are always face down. Always.
In the nightmare, the Dealer has your face. He deals with your hands. He smiles with your mouth.
You sit across from yourself and lose every hand. You can't beat yourself. You never could.
You wake up and avoid the mirror for the rest of the morning.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.

Waving the vehicle down, the truck slows, then halts, and an old, jolly man jumps out.
"Well, howdy! The name's Tom. It appears you've gotten yourself in a bit of a pickle, ya think?"
Tom pulls a big red wrench out of his pocket, and walks to the hood of your beaten down wagon.
"Yep, this thing's busted alright! Tell ya what, for, I don't know, 350 bucks,
I'll get this thing replaced for ya, good as new! Whaddya say?"
"Aww man, sorry to tell you, but you just don't got enough funds for this, yunno?"
"You know what? I'm feelin' generous, and the shop's been doing well lately.
Tell ya what, I can take the offer down 50 dollars just for you.
Could ya do 300 bucks?"
"Still can't afford it? That's a real shame. I really wish there was something I could do. Best of luck my friend. Be seeing ya around, ya know?"
And with that, you watch as his big golden truck stutters, starts, then drives away.
As you're walking along the side of the road, something catches your eye. A flash of white, darting between the bushes. A rabbit!
Without thinking, you give chase. The little creature bounds ahead of you, zigging and zagging through the underbrush with seemingly effortless grace.
You run and run, but no matter how fast you go, the rabbit stays just out of reach. Its white tail bobs mockingly in the moonlight.
Finally, you stop, hands on your knees, gasping for breath. When you look up, the rabbit is gone, vanished into the night like it was never there.
You trudge back to your wagon, defeated. But something tells you this isn't the last time you'll see that rabbit.

The Dealer stops shuffling. He looks at you with those dark eyes.
For a moment, he's not the Dealer. He's just a man with a memory that hurts.
"Some cards aren't meant to be played. Let's leave it at that."
He resumes shuffling. The moment is over. But the tremor isn't.
For the rest of the session, the Dealer plays differently. Quieter.
